title: C errno
content:
### errno in C

`errno` is a global variable (or macro) defined in `<errno.h>` that stores error codes from system calls and library functions.

### How errno Works

- Set to 0 at program startup
- Library functions set `errno` to a non-zero value on error
- Should be reset to 0 before calling a function if you want to check
- Not thread-safe by default (use `<errno.h>` local variant)

### Common errno Values

| Macro | Value | Meaning |
|-------|-------|---------|
| `EPERM` | 1 | Operation not permitted |
| `ENOENT` | 2 | No such file/directory |
| `ESRCH` | 3 | No such process |
| `EINTR` | 4 | Interrupted function |
| `EIO` | 5 | I/O error |
| `ENXIO` | 6 | No such device/address |
| `E2BIG` | 7 | Argument list too long |
| `ENOMEM` | 12 | Out of memory |
| `EACCES` | 13 | Permission denied |
| `EEXIST` | 17 | File exists |
| `EINVAL` | 22 | Invalid argument |
| `ERANGE` | 34 | Result too large |

### Useful Functions

| Function | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `perror(str)` | Prints `str: error_message` to stderr |
| `strerror(errno)` | Returns error description string |

---

> **Key Point:** Always check `errno` immediately after a function fails. A successful function call may also set errno (without clearing it), so check it only when an error is indicated.

quiz.question: Which function prints a human-readable error based on errno?
